flashcards.definition: All the organisms living in an area plus the nonliving physical components (soil, water, atmosphere) with which they interact.
Biotic factors are living things (plants, animals, bacteria); abiotic factors are nonliving (temperature, light, water, soil, pH).
Energy flows from producers → primary consumers → secondary consumers → tertiary consumers; only ~10% of energy transfers between levels (energy pyramid).
CO₂ is removed from atmosphere by photosynthesis, stored in organisms, returned to atmosphere through respiration and decomposition, and cycled through fossil fuels.
Nitrogen gas (N₂) is converted to usable form by nitrogen-fixing bacteria, taken up by plants, passed through food chains, and returned to atmosphere by denitrifying bacteria.
Water evaporates from oceans/lakes, transpires from plants, condenses into clouds, precipitates as rain/snow, and returns to water bodies via runoff and infiltration.
Producer: autotrophs that convert sunlight to chemical energy (plants, algae); Primary consumer: herbivore eating producers; Secondary consumer: carnivore eating primary consumers.
Organisms (bacteria, fungi) that break down dead organic matter and return nutrients to the soil; essential for nutrient cycling.
The variety of species, genetic information, and ecosystems in an area; greater biodiversity increases ecosystem resilience and stability.
The gradual change in species composition in an area over time; primary succession occurs on bare rock/new land; secondary succession follows disturbance in established ecosystems.
